title: The Exit Plan
subtitle: Lock-in, off-boarding & contingency

hook.metaphor: Before you move in with someone, the wise question isn't just 'how good is this?' but 'how hard would it be to leave?' A relationship you *can't* exit isn't a partnership — it's a trap. With vendors it's the same: if leaving a supplier would cripple you, they own you, and any failure of theirs becomes your hostage situation.
hook.scenario: A company built everything on one vendor's proprietary platform. The vendor triples its prices and lets service slide. The company does the maths on leaving — and discovers untangling would take two years and cost a fortune. They're trapped. When should they have thought about the exit?

learn: **Vendor lock-in** is when leaving a supplier is so costly or difficult that you're effectively stuck — and a stuck customer has no leverage over price, quality, or risk. Our trapped company is paying the price of never planning an exit.

The fix is counter-intuitive: **plan the exit before you enter.** When you onboard a vendor, you ask how you'd off-board them — and you bake the answer into the contract: data must be returned in a usable format, reasonable notice periods, transition help, and no hostage-taking of *your* data.

For critical vendors you also need a **contingency / continuity plan**: if this supplier failed *tomorrow*, what would we do? Is there an alternative? A manual fallback? Knowing the answer turns a vendor collapse from an existential crisis into a manageable switch — the resilience mindset from Operational Risk, applied to suppliers.

Watch for things that deepen lock-in: **proprietary formats** (your data trapped in a shape only they can read), deep integration with no alternatives, and letting a vendor hold the only copy of critical data. The more unique and entangled the dependency, the harder the exit.

And remember off-boarding is also a **security** event: when a relationship ends, revoke the vendor's access promptly and confirm they've securely deleted or returned your data. A 'former' vendor who still has live access and your data is a breach waiting to happen.
bridge: So at work, you can ask the question most teams skip during the excitement of signing: *if this vendor failed or turned hostile, how hard would it be to leave?* Negotiate data portability and exit terms up front, keep a contingency plan for critical suppliers, and treat off-boarding (revoke access, retrieve data) as carefully as onboarding. An exit you've planned is leverage; one you haven't is a trap.

interactions.title: Trapped on the platform
interactions.prompt: A company is locked into a vendor that hiked prices and dropped service; leaving would take two years. When should the exit have been considered?

options.label: Now — just start the painful two-year exit.
options.feedback: They may have to — but the real lesson is timing. Had they planned portability and exit terms at the start, they'd have leverage now instead of a two-year ordeal.

options.label: At the beginning — exit terms and data portability belong in the original deal.
options.feedback: Exactly. You plan the exit before you enter. Contractual data portability, notice periods, and a contingency plan would have turned this trap into a manageable switch.

options.label: Never — loyalty to one vendor is always best.
options.feedback: Blind loyalty with no exit is precisely lock-in. Loyalty is fine; having no way out is what hands the vendor total power over you.

interactions.title: Deepens or eases lock-in?
interactions.prompt: Sort each factor by whether it makes leaving a vendor harder or easier.

items.label: Your data trapped in a proprietary format only they read
Contractual right to export data in a standard format
The vendor holds the only copy of critical data
A documented contingency plan with an alternative provider
Deep, custom integration with no alternatives
Agreed transition assistance and notice periods

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Always plan the exit before you enter — negotiate data portability and exit terms up front and keep a contingency plan — because a vendor you can't leave owns you, and any failure of theirs becomes your trap.”

options: Being unable to leave removes your leverage over price, quality, and risk
checkpoint.explain: A customer who can't leave is a hostage — the vendor controls the terms and any failure traps you.

checkpoint.q: The best time to plan a vendor exit is…
options: Before you enter — bake portability and exit terms into the contract
checkpoint.explain: Exit terms, data portability, and a contingency plan negotiated up front give you leverage later.

checkpoint.q: Off-boarding a vendor is also a security task because you must…
options: Revoke their access and confirm your data is returned or securely deleted
Leave their access on just in case
checkpoint.explain: A former vendor still holding live access and your data is a breach waiting to happen.
